entries.title: Isaiah — Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-18. (Compare Margin). The prosperity of the wicked is short.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:1–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-21. guide ... way-- or direct thy thoughts to a right course of conduct (compare Pr 4:4; 9:6).

entries.title: Isaiah 1:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. Hearken-- that is, obey (Pr 1:8; Ep 6:1). despise ... old-- Adults revere the parents whom, as children, they once obeyed.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. Buy-- literally, "get" (Pr 4:5). truth-- generally and specially as opposed to errors of all kinds.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 1:8–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26-35. A solemn warning against whoredom and drunkenness (Ho 4:11). give me-- This is the address of that divine wisdom so often presented (Pr 8:1; 9:3, &c.). heart-- confidence. observe-- keep. my ways-- such as I teach you (Pr 3:17; 9:6).

entries.title: Isaiah 1:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 24 Pr 24:1-34. 1-2. (Compare Pr 23:3, 17; Psa 37:1).

entries.title: Isaiah 1:21–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. (Compare Pr 14:1; Is 54:14). house-- including the family.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The general statement (Ec 9:16, 18) is specially illustrated (compare Pr 21:22; Psa 144:1).

entries.title: Isaiah 1:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. (Compare Pr 14:16). in the gate-- (Compare Pr 22:22).

entries.title: Isaiah 1:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. So called even if he fails to do evil.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Same thought varied.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Literally, "If thou fail in the day of straits (adversity), strait (or, small) is thy strength," which is then truly tested.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:29–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. Neglect of known duty is sin (Jas 4:17). ready-- literally, "bowing down" to be slain-- that is, unjustly. God's retributive justice cannot be avoided by professed ignorance.

entries.title: Isaiah 1:31–2:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. As delicious food whets the appetite, so should the rewards of wisdom excite us to seek it.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. The plots of the wicked against the good, though partially, shall not be fully successful (Psa 37:24); while the wicked, falling under penal evil, find no help.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-18. Yet let none rejoice over the fate of evildoers, lest God punish their wrong spirit by relieving the sufferer (compare Pr 17:5; Job 31:29).

entries.title: Isaiah 2:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 2:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. A warning against impiety and resistance to lawful rule (Ro 13:1-7; 1Pe 2:17). meddle ... change-- (Compare Margin), literally, "mingle not yourself," avoid the society of restless persons.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. These ... wise-- literally, "are of the wise," as authors (compare "Psalms of David," Hebrew). "These" refers to the verses following, Pr 24:24-34. to have respect-- literally, "to discern faces," show partiality,

entries.title: Isaiah 2:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24-25. of which an example is justifying the wicked, to which is opposed, rebuking him, which has a blessing.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. kiss his lips-- love and obey, do homage (Psa 2:12; So 8:1). right answer-- literally, "plain words" (compare Pr 8:9), opposed to deceptive, or obscure.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. Prepare ... in the field-- Secure, by diligence, a proper support, and then build; provide necessaries, then comforts, to which a house rather pertained, in a mild climate, permitting the use of tents.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. Do not speak even truth needlessly against any, and never falsehood.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 29. Especially avoid retaliation (Mt 5:43-45; Ro 12:17).

entries.title: Isaiah 2:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30-31. A striking picture of the effects of sloth.

entries.title: Isaiah 2:18–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-34. From the folly of the sluggard learn wisdom (Pr 6:10, 11).

entries.title: Isaiah 2:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 25 Pr 25:1-28. 1. The character of these proverbs sustains the title (see Introduction). also-- refers to the former part of the book. copied out-- literally, "transferred," that is, from some other book to this; not given from memory.

entries.title: Isaiah 3 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. God's unsearchableness impresses us with awe (compare Is 45:15; Ro 11:33). But kings, being finite, should confer with wise counsellors;

entries.title: Isaiah 3:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Ye wisely keeping state secrets, which to common men are as inaccessible heights and depths.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. As separating impurities from ore leaves pure silver, so taking from a king wicked counsellors leaves a wise and beneficent government.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. Do not intrude into the presence of the king, for the elevation of the humble is honorable, but the humbling of the proud disgraceful (Lu 14:8-10).

entries.title: Isaiah 3:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. (Compare Pr 3:30). lest ... shame-- lest you do what you ought not, when shamed by defeat, or "lest thou art shut out from doing any thing."

entries.title: Isaiah 3:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-10. (Compare Mt 5:25, Margin). secret-- that is, of your opponent, for his disadvantage, and so you be disgraced, not having discussed your difficulties with him.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. a word fitly-- literally, "quickly," as wheels roll, just in time. The comparison as apples ... silver gives a like sense. apples, &c.-- either real apples of golden color, in a silver network basket, or imitations on silver embroidery.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Those who desire to know and do rightly, most highly esteem good counsel (Pr 9:9; 15:31). The listening ear is better than one hung with gold.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Snow from mountains was used to cool drinks; so refreshing is a faithful messenger (Pr 13:17).

entries.title: Isaiah 3:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. clouds-- literally, "vapors" (Jr 10:13), clouds only in appearance. a false gift-- promised, but not given.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Gentleness and kindness overcome the most powerful and obstinate. long forbearing-- or, "slowness to anger" (Pr 14:29; 15:18).

entries.title: Isaiah 3:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. A comparison, as a surfeit of honey produces physical disgust, so your company, however agreeable in moderation, may, if excessive, lead your friend to hate you.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. A false witness is as destructive to reputation, as such weapons to the body (Pr 24:28). beareth ... witness-- literally, "answereth questions," as before a judge, against his neighbor.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. Treachery annoys as well as deceives.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. Not only is the incongruity of songs (that is, joyful) and sadness meant, but an accession of sadness, by want of sympathy, is implied.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-22. (Compare Mt 5:44; Ro 12:20). As metals are melted by heaping coals upon them, so is the heart softened by kindness.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. Better, "As the north wind bringeth forth (Psa 90:2) or produces rain, so does a concealed or slandering tongue produce anger."

entries.title: Isaiah 3:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 3:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. (Compare Pr 25:13). good news-- that is, of some loved interest or absent friend, the more grateful as coming from afar.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. From troubled fountains and corrupt springs no healthy water is to be had, so when the righteous are oppressed by the wicked, their power for good is lessened or destroyed.

entries.title: Isaiah 3:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. Satiety surfeits (Pr 25:16); so men who are self-glorious find shame. is not glory-- "not" is supplied from the first clause, or "is grievous," in which sense a similar word is used (Pr 27:2).

entries.title: Isaiah 3:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. Such are exposed to the incursions of evil thoughts and successful temptations.

entries.title: Isaiah 4:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 26 Pr 26:1-28. 1. The incongruities of nature illustrate also those of the moral world. The fool's unworthiness is also implied (Pr 17:7; 19:10).

entries.title: Isaiah 4:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Though not obvious to us, the bird-- literally, "sparrow"--and swallow-- have an object in their motions, so penal evil falls on none without a reason.

entries.title: Isaiah 4:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. The rod is as much needed by fools and as well suited to them, as whips and bridles are for beasts.

entries.title: Isaiah 4:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. Answer not-- that is, approvingly by like folly.

entries.title: Isaiah 4:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. A fool fails by folly as surely as if he were maimed. drinketh damage-- that is, gets it abundantly (Job 15:16; 34:7).

entries.title: Isaiah 5 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. legs ... equal-- or, "take away the legs," or "the legs ... are weak." In any case the idea is that they are the occasion of an awkwardness, such as the fool shows in using a parable or proverb (see Introduction; Pr 17:7).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. A stone, bound in a sling, is useless; so honor, conferred on a fool, is thrown away.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. As vexatious and unmanageable as a thorn in a drunkard's hand is a parable to a fool. He will be as apt to misuse is as to use it rightly.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Various versions of this are proposed (compare Margin). Better perhaps-- "Much He injures (or literally, "wounds") all who reward," &c., that is, society is injured by encouraging evil men. transgressors-- may be rendered "vagrants." The word "God" is improperly supplied.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. returneth ... folly-- Though disgusting to others, the fool delights in his folly.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. The self-conceited are taught with more difficulty than the stupid.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Pr 22:13).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 5:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. (Compare Pr 19:24).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. The thoughtless being ignorant of their ignorance are conceited.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. meddleth-- as in Pr 20:19; 24:21; as either holding a dog by the ears or letting him go involves danger, so success in another man's strife or failure involves a useless risk of reputation, does no good, and may do us harm.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:11–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-19. Such are reckless of results.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-21. The talebearers foster (Pr 16:28), and the contentious excite, strife.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. (Compare Pr 18:8).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. Warm professions can no more give value to insincerity than silver coating to rude earthenware.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. dissembleth-- though an unusual sense of the word (compare Margin), is allowable, and better suits the context, which sets forth hypocrisy.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. Sentiment of Pr 26:24 carried out. seven abominations in his heart-- that is, very many (compare Pr 24:16).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26-27. Deceit will at last be exposed, and the wicked by their own arts often bring on retribution (compare Pr 12:13; Psa 7:16; 9:17, &c.).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. Men hate those they injure. A lying tongue-- "lips" for the persons (compare Pr 4:24; Psa 12:3).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 27 Pr 27:1-27. 1. Do not confide implicitly in your plans (Pr 16:9; 19:21; Jas 4:13-15).

entries.title: Isaiah 5:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Avoid self-praise.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. heavy-- The literal sense of "heavy," applied to material subjects, illustrates its figurative, "grievous," applied to moral. a fool's wrath-- is unreasonable and excessive.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. envy-- or, "jealousy" (compare Margin; Pr 6:34), is more unappeasable than the simpler bad passions.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:27–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. secret love-- not manifested in acts is useless; and even, if its exhibition by rebukes wounds us, such love is preferable to the frequent (compare Margin), and hence deceitful, kisses of an enemy.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The luxury of wealth confers less happiness than the healthy appetite of labor.

entries.title: Isaiah 5:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Such are not only out of place, but out of duty and in danger.

entries.title: Isaiah 6 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. rejoice the heart-- the organ of perceiving what pleases the senses. sweetness ... counsel-- or, "wise counsel is also pleasing."

entries.title: Isaiah 6:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Adhere to tried friends. The ties of blood may be less reliable than those of genuine friendship.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. The wisdom of children both reflects credit on parents and contributes to their aid in difficulties.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 6:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. Excessive zeal in praising raises suspicions of selfishness.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. (Compare Pr 19:13). very ... day-- literally, "a day of showers."

entries.title: Isaiah 6:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. hideth-- or, "restrains" (that is, tries to do it); is as fruitless an effort, as that of holding the wind. the ointment of his right hand-- the organ of power (Psa 17:7; 18:35). His right hand endeavors to repress perfume, but vainly. Some prefer: "His right hand comes on oil," that is, "cannot take hold." Such a woman cannot be tamed.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. a man sharpeneth ... friend-- that is, conversation promotes intelligence, which the face exhibits.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. Diligence secures a reward, even for the humble servant.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. We may see our characters in the developed tempers of others.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. Men's cupidity is as insatiable as the grave.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. Praise tests character. a man to his praise-- according to his praise, as he bears it. Thus vain men seek it, weak men are inflated by it, wise men disregard it, &c.

entries.title: Isaiah 6:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. The obstinate wickedness of such is incurable by the heaviest inflictions.

entries.title: Isaiah 7 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23-24. flocks-- constituted the staple of wealth. It is only by care and diligence that the most solid possessions can be perpetuated (Pr 23:5).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:2–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25-27. The fact that providential arrangements furnish the means of competence to those who properly use them is another motive to diligence (compare Psa 65:9-13). The hay appeareth-- literally, "Grass appeareth" (Job 40:15; Psa 104:14).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 28 Pr 28:1-28. 1. A bad conscience makes men timid; the righteous are alone truly bold (Pr 14:26; Psa 27:1).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Anarchy producing contending rulers shortens the reign of each. but by a man ... prolonged-- or, "by a man of understanding--that is, a good ruler--he who knows or regards the right, that is, a good citizen, shall prolong (his days)." Good rulers are a blessing to the people. Bad government as a punishment for evil is contrasted with good as blessing to the good.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. A poor man, &c.-- Such, in power, exact more severely, and so leave subjects bare.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. They that forsake ... wicked-- Wrongdoers encourage one another.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. (Compare Joh 7:17). Ignorance of moral truth is due to unwillingness to know it.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. (Compare Pr 10:6). Riches cannot compensate for sin, nor the want of them affect integrity.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. (Compare Pr 17:25). riotous men-- or, "gluttons" (Pr 23:20, 21).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. usury ... unjust gain-- (Compare Margin). The two terms, meaning nearly the same, may denote excessive interest. God's providence directs the proper use of wealth.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. (Compare Pr 15:8; 21:27). hearing-- that is, obeying. God requires sincere worshippers (Psa 66:18; Joh 4:24).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. (Compare Pr 26:27).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. A poor but wise man can discover (and expose) the rich and self-conceited.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. great glory-- or, cause for it to a people, for the righteous rejoice in good, and righteousness exalts a nation (Pr 14:34). a man ... hidden-- that is, the good retire, or all kinds try to escape a wicked rule.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Psa 32:3-5). Concealment of sin delivers none from God's wrath, but He shows mercy to the humble penitent (Psa 51:4).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. feareth-- that is, God, and so repents. hardeneth his heart-- makes himself insensible to sin, and so will not repent (Pr 14:16; 29:1).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. The rapacity and cruelty of such beasts well represent some wicked men (compare Psa 7:2; 17:12).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. The prince ... understanding-- that is, He does not perceive that oppression jeopards his success. Covetousness often produces oppression, hence the contrast.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. doeth violence ... blood, &c.-- or, that is oppressed by the blood of life (Ge 9:6), which he has taken. to the pit-- the grave or destruction (Pr 1:12; Job 33:18-24; Psa 143:7). stay him-- sustain or deliver him.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. (Compare Pr 10:9; 17:20). Double dealing is eventually fatal.

entries.title: Isaiah 7:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. (Compare Pr 10:4; 20:4). vain persons-- idle, useless drones, implying that they are also wicked (Pr 12:11; Psa 26:4).

entries.title: Isaiah 7:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. maketh haste ... rich-- implying deceit or fraud (Pr 20:21), and so opposed to "faithful" or reliable.

entries.title: Isaiah 8 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. respect of persons-- (Pr 24:23). Such are led to evil by the slightest motive.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. (Compare Pr 28:20). evil eye-- in the general sense of Pr 23:6, here more specific for covetousness (compare Pr 22:9; Mt 20:15). poverty ... him-- by God's providence.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. (Compare Pr 9:8, 9; 27:5). Those benefited by reproof will love their monitors.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. (Compare Mt 15:4-6). Such, though heirs, are virtually thieves, to be ranked with highwaymen.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. of a proud heart-- literally, "puffed up of soul"--that is, self-confident, and hence overbearing and litigious. made fat-- or, "prosperous" (Pr 11:25; 16:20).

entries.title: Isaiah 8:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. (Compare Pr 3:6-8). walketh wisely-- that is, trusting in God (Pr 22:17-19).

entries.title: Isaiah 8:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. (Compare Pr 11:24-26). hideth his eyes-- as the face (Psa 27:9; 69:17), denotes inattention.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. The elevation of the wicked to power drives men to seek refuge from tyranny (compare Pr 28:12; 11:10; Psa 12:8).

entries.title: Isaiah 8:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 29 Pr 29:1-27. 1. hardeneth ... neck-- obstinately refuses counsel (2Ki 17:14; Ne 9:16). destroyed-- literally, "shivered" or "utterly broken to pieces." without remedy-- literally, "without healing" or repairing.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. (Compare Pr 11:10; 28:28). in authority-- (Compare Margin), increased in power.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 8:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. by judgment-- that is, righteous decisions, opposed to those procured by gifts (compare Pr 28:21), by which good government is perverted. land-- for nation.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. (Compare Pr 26:28). spreadeth ... feet-- By misleading him as to his real character, the flatterer brings him to evil, prepared by himself or others.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. In-- or, "By" the transgression-- he is brought into difficulty (Pr 12:13), but the righteous go on prospering, and so sing or rejoice.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. considereth-- literally, "knows," as Psa 1:6. the cause-- that is, in courts of justice (compare Pr 29:14). The voluntary neglect of it by the wicked (Pr 28:27) occasions oppression.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Scornful men-- those who contemptuously disregard God's law. bring-- (Compare Margin), kindle strife. turn away wrath-- that is, "abate wrath."

entries.title: Isaiah 8:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. contendeth-- that is, in law. whether ... laugh-- The fool, whether angry or good-humored, is unsettled; or referring the words to the wise man, the sense is, that all his efforts, severe or gentle, are unavailing to pacify the fool.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. bloodthirsty-- (Compare Margin), murderers (Psa 5:6; 26:9). hate, &c.-- (Pr 1:11; Ge 3:4). seek ... soul-- that is, to preserve it.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. (Compare Pr 12:16; 16:32). mind-- or, "spirit," for anger or any ill passion which the righteous restrain.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. His servants imitate him.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Pr 22:2). deceitful man-- literally, "man of vexations," an exactor. the Lord ... their eyes-- sustains their lives (1Sa 14:27; Psa 13:3); that is, both depend on Him, and He will do justice.

entries.title: Isaiah 8:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. (Compare Pr 20:28; 25:5). Such is the character of the King of kings (Psa 72:4, 12).

entries.title: Isaiah 9 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 9:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. (Compare Pr 29:2, 12; Psa 12:1-8). shall see ... fall-- and triumph in it (Psa 37:34-38; 58:10, 11).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. (Compare Pr 29:3, 15; Pr 19:18). give thee rest-- peace and quiet (compare Pr 29:9).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. no vision-- instruction in God's truth, which was by prophets, through visions (1Sa 3:1). people perish-- (Compare Margin), are deprived of moral restraints. keepeth the law-- has, and observes, instruction (Pr 14:11, 34; Psa 19:11).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. A servant-- who lacks good principle. corrected-- or discovered. will not answer-- that is, will not obey.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. (Compare Pr 21:5). hasty in ... words?-- implying self-conceit (Pr 26:12).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. become his son-- assume the place and privileges of one.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. (Compare Pr 15:18). Such are delighted by discord and violence.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. (Compare Pr 16:18; 18:12). honour ... spirit-- or, "such shall lay hold on honor" (Pr 11:16).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. hateth ... soul-- (Compare Pr 8:36). heareth cursing-- (Le 5:1), risks the punishment, rather than reveal truth.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. The fear ... snare-- involves men in difficulty (compare Pr 29:6). shall be safe-- (Compare Margin; Pr 18:10).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. (Compare Margin; Psa 27:8). God alone will and can do exact justice.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. (Compare Pr 3:32). On last clause, compare Pr 29:16; Psa 37:12.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 30 Pr 30:1-33. 1. This is the title of this chapter (see Introduction). the prophecy-- literally, "the burden" (compare Is 13:1; Zec 9:1), used for any divine instruction; not necessarily a prediction, which was only a kind of prophecy (1Ch 15:27, "a song"). Prophets were inspired men, who spoke for God to man, or for man to God (Ge 20:7; Ex 7:14, 15, 16). Such, also, were the New Testament prophets. In a general sense, Gad, Nathan, and others were such, who were divine teachers, though we do not learn that they ever predicted. the man spake-- literally, "the saying of the man"; an expression used to denote any solemn and important announcement (compare 2Sa 23:1; Psa 36:1; 110:1; Is 1:24, &c.). Ithiel and Ucal were perhaps pupils.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:15–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-4. brutish-- stupid, a strong term to denote his lowly self-estimation; or he may speak of such as his natural condition, as contrasted with God's all-seeing comprehensive knowledge and almighty power. The questions of this clause emphatically deny the attributes mentioned to be those of any creature, thus impressively strengthening the implied reference of the former to God (compare De 30:12-14; Is 40:12; Ep 4:8).

entries.title: Isaiah 9:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 9:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Add ... words-- implying that his sole reliance was on God's all-sufficient teaching. reprove thee-- or, "convict thee"--and so the falsehood will appear.

entries.title: Isaiah 9:20–10:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-9. A prayer for exemption from wickedness, and the extremes of poverty and riches, the two things mentioned. Contentment is implied as desired.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Accuse not-- Slander not (Psa 10:7). curse ... guilty-- lest, however lowly, he be exasperated to turn on thee, and your guilt be made to appear.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:2–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-14. Four kinds of hateful persons-- (1) graceless children, (2) hypocrites, (3) the proud, (4) cruel oppressors (compare on Pr 30:14; Psa 14:4; 52:2)--are now illustrated; (1) Pr 30:15, 16, the insatiability of prodigal children and their fate; (2) Pr 30:17, hypocrisy, or the concealment of real character; (3 and 4) Pr 30:18-20, various examples of pride and oppression.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. horse leech-- supposed by some to be the vampire (a fabulous creature), as being literally insatiable; but the other subjects mentioned must be taken as this, comparatively insatiable. The use of a fabulous creature agreeably to popular notions is not inconsistent with inspiration. There are three ... yea, four-- (Compare Pr 6:16).

entries.title: Isaiah 10:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. The eye-- for the person, with reference to the use of the organ to express mockery and contempt, and also as that by which punishment is received. the ravens ... eagles ... eat-- either as dying unnaturally, or being left unburied, or both.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:9–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-20. Hypocrisy is illustrated by four examples of the concealment of all methods or traces of action, and a pertinent example of double dealing in actual vice is added, that is, the adulterous woman.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:12–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-23. Pride and cruelty, the undue exaltation of those unfit to hold power, produce those vices which disquiet society (compare Pr 19:10; 28:3).

entries.title: Isaiah 10:15–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24-31. These verses provide two classes of apt illustrations of various aspects of the moral world, which the reader is left to apply. By the first (Pr 30:25-28), diligence and providence are commended; the success of these insignificant animals being due to their instinctive sagacity and activity, rather than strength. The other class (Pr 30:30, 31) provides similes for whatever is majestic or comely, uniting efficiency with gracefulness.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32. As none can hope, successfully, to resist such a king, suppress even the thought of an attempt. lay ... hand upon thy mouth-- "lay" is well supplied (Jud 18:19; Job 29:9; 40:4).

entries.title: Isaiah 10:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 33. That is, strife-- or other ills, as surely arise from devising evil as natural effects from natural causes.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 31 Pr 31:1-31. 1. On the title of this, the sixth part of the book, see Introduction. prophecy-- (See on Pr 30:1).

entries.title: Isaiah 10:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. What, my son?-- that is, What shall I say? Repetitions denote earnestness. son of my womb-- as our phrase, "my own son," a term of special affection. son of my vows-- as one dedicated to God; so the word "Lemuel" may mean.

entries.title: Isaiah 10:28–34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-9. Succinct but solemn warnings against vices to which kings are peculiarly tempted, as carnal pleasures and oppressive and unrighteous government are used to sustain sensual indulgence. strength-- mental and bodily resources for health and comfort. thy ways-- or course of life. to that ... kings-- literally, "to the destroying of kings," avoid destructive pleasures (compare Pr 5:9; 7:22, 27; Ho 4:11).

entries.title: Isaiah 11 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-31. This exquisite picture of a truly lovely wife is conceived and drawn in accordance with the customs of Eastern nations, but its moral teachings suit all climes. In Hebrew the verses begin with the letters of the Hebrew alphabet in order (compare Introduction to Poetical Books). Who ... woman-- The question implies that such are rare, though not entirely wanting (compare Pr 18:22; 19:14). virtuous-- literally, "of strength," that is, moral courage (compare Pr 12:4; Ru 3:11). her price, &c.-- (compare Pr 3:15).

entries.title: Isaiah 12:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: ECCLESIASTES; OR THE PREACHER. THE GREEK TITLE IN THE LXX.

entries.title: Isaiah 13 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: INTRODUCTION The Hebrew title is Koheleth, which the speaker in it applies to himself (Ec 1:12), "I, Koheleth, was king over Israel." It means an Assembler or Convener of a meeting and a Preacher to such a meeting. The feminine form of the Hebrew noun, and its construction once (Ec 7:27) with a feminine verb, show that it not only signifies Solomon, the Preacher to assemblies (in which case it is construed with the verb or noun masculine), but also Divine Wisdom (feminine in Hebrew) speaking by the mouth of the inspired king. In six cases out of seven it is construed with the masculine. Solomon was endowed with inspired wisdom (1Ki 3:5-14; 6:11, 12; 9:1-9; 11:9-11), specially fitting him for the task. The Orientals delight in such meetings for grave discourse. Thus the Arabs formerly had an assembly yearly, at Ocadh, for hearing and reciting poems. Compare "Masters of assemblies" (see on Ec 12:11, also Ec 12:9). "The Preacher taught the people knowledge," probably viva voce ("orally"); 1Ki 4:34; 10:2, 8, 24; 2Ch 9:1, 7, 23, plainly refer to a somewhat public divan met for literary discussion. So "spake," thrice repeated (1Ki 4:32, 33), refers not to written compositions, but to addresses spoken in assemblies convened for the purpose. The Holy Ghost, no doubt, signifies also by the term that Solomon's doctrine is intended for the "great congregation," the Church of all places and ages (Psa 22:25; 49:2-4). Solomon was plainly the author (Ec 1:12, 16; 2:15; 12:9). That the Rabbins attribute it to Isaiah or Hezekiah is explicable by supposing that one or the other inserted it in the canon. The difference of its style, as compared with Proverbs and Song of Solomon, is due to the difference of subjects, and the different period of his life in which each was written; the Song, in the fervor of his first love to God; Proverbs, about the same time, or somewhat later; but Ecclesiastes in late old age, as the seal and testimony of repentance of his apostasy in the intervening period: Psa 89:30, 33 proves his penitence. The substitution of the title Koheleth for Solomon (that is, peace), may imply that, having troubled Israel, meantime he forfeited his name of peace (1Ki 11:14, 23); but now, having repented, he wishes to be henceforth a Preacher of righteousness. The alleged foreign expressions in the Hebrew may have been easily imported, through the great intercourse there was with other nations during his long reign. Moreover, supposed Chaldaisms may be fragments preserved from the common tongue of which Hebrew, Syriac, Chaldee, and Arabic were offshoots. The Scope of Ecclesiastes is to show the vanity of all mere human pursuits, when made the chief end, as contrasted with the real blessedness of true wisdom, that is, religion. The immortality of the soul is dwelt on incidentally, as subsidiary to the main scope. Moses' law took this truth for granted but drew its sanctions of rewards and punishments in accordance with the theocracy, which was under a special providence of God as the temporal King of Israel, from the present life, rather than the future. But after Israel chose an earthly king, God withdrew, in part, His extraordinary providence, so that under Solomon, temporal rewards did not invariably follow virtue, and punishments vice (compare Ec 2:16; 3:19; 4:1; 5:8; 7:15; 8:14; 9:2, 11). Hence the need arises to show that these anomalies will be rectified hereafter, and this is the grand "conclusion," therefore, of the "whole" book, that, seeing there is a coming judgment, and seeing that present goods do not satisfy the soul, "man's whole duty is to fear God and keep his commandments" (Ec 12:13, 14), and meanwhile, to use, in joyful and serene sobriety, and not abuse, the present life (Ec 3:12, 13). It is objected that sensual epicurism seems to be inculcated (Ec 3:12, 13, 22, &c.); but it is a contented, thankful enjoyment of God's present gifts that is taught, as opposed to a murmuring, anxious, avaricious spirit, as is proved by Ec 5:18, compare with Ec 5:11-15, not making them the chief end of life; not the joy of levity and folly; a misunderstanding which he guards against in Ec 7:2-6; 11:9; 12:1. Again, Ec 7:16; 9:2-10, might seem to teach fatalism and skepticism. But these are words put in the mouth of an objector; or rather, they were the language of Solomon himself during his apostasy, finding an echo in the heart of every sensualist, who wishes to be an unbeliever, and, who, therefore, sees difficulties enough in the world around wherewith to prop up his wilful unbelief. The answer is given (Ec 7:17, 18; 9:11, 12; 11:1, 6; 12:13). Even if these passages be taken as words of Solomon, they are to be understood as forbidding a self-made "righteousness," which tries to constrain God to grant salvation to imaginary good works and external strictness with which it wearies itself; also, that speculation which tries to fathom all God's inscrutable counsels (Ec 8:17), and that carefulness about the future forbidden in Mt 6:25. The Chief Good is that the possession of that which makes us happy, is to be sought as the end, for its own sake; whereas, all other things are but means towards it. Philosophers, who made it the great subject of inquiry, restricted it to the present life, treating the eternal as unreal, and only useful to awe the multitude with. But Solomon shows the vanity of all human things (so-called philosophy included) to satisfy the soul, and that heavenly wisdom alone is the chief good. He had taught so when young (Pr 1:20; 8:1); so also; in Song of Solomon, he had spiritualized the subject in an allegory; and now, after having long personally tried the manifold ways in which the worldly seek to reach happiness, he gives the fruit of his experience in old age. It is divided into two parts-- Ec 1:1-6:10 showing the vanity of earthly things; Ec 6:10-12:14, the excellence of heavenly wisdom. Deviations from strict logical methods occur in these divisions, but in the main they are observed. The deviations make it the less stiff and artificial, and the more suited to all capacities. It is in poetry; the hemistichal division is mostly observed, but occasionally not so. The choice of epithets, imagery, inverted order of words, ellipses, parallelism, or, in its absence, similarity of diction, mark versification. CHAPTER 1 Ec 1:1-18. Introduction. 1. the Preacher-- and Convener of assemblies for the purpose. See my Preface. Koheleth in Hebrew, a symbolical name for Solomon, and of Heavenly Wisdom speaking through and identified with him. Ec 1:12 shows that "king of Jerusalem" is in apposition, not with "David," but "Preacher." of Jerusalem-- rather, "in Jerusalem," for it was merely his metropolis, not his whole kingdom.

entries.title: Isaiah 13:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. The theme proposed of the first part of his discourse. Vanity of vanities-- Hebraism for the most utter vanity. So "holy of holies" (Ex 26:33); "servant of servants" (Ge 9:25). The repetition increases the force. all-- Hebrew, "the all"; all without exception, namely, earthly things. vanity-- not in themselves, for God maketh nothing in vain (1Ti 4:4, 5), but vain when put in the place of God and made the end, instead of the means (Psa 39:5, 6; 62:9; Mt 6:33); vain, also, because of the "vanity" to which they are "subjected" by the fall (Ro 8:20).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. What profit ... labour-- that is, "What profit" as to the chief good (Mt 16:26). Labor is profitable in its proper place (Ge 2:15; 3:19; Pr 14:23). under the sun-- that is, in this life, as opposed to the future world. The phrase often recurs, but only in Ecclesiastes.

entries.title: Isaiah 13:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. earth ... for ever-- (Psa 104:5). While the earth remains the same, the generations of men are ever changing; what lasting profit, then, can there be from the toils of one whose sojourn on earth, as an individual, is so brief? The "for ever" is comparative, not absolute (Psa 102:26).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. (Psa 19:5, 6). "Panting" as the Hebrew for "hasteth"; metaphor, from a runner (Psa 19:5, "a strong man") in a "race." It applies rather to the rising sun, which seems laboriously to mount up to the meridian, than to the setting sun; the accents too favor Maurer, "And (that too, returning) to his place, where panting he riseth."

entries.title: Isaiah 13:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. according to his circuits-- that is, it returns afresh to its former circuits, however many be its previous veerings about. The north and south winds are the two prevailing winds in Palestine and Egypt.

entries.title: Isaiah 13:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. By subterraneous cavities, and by evaporation forming rain clouds, the fountains and rivers are supplied from the sea, into which they then flow back. The connection is: Individual men are continually changing, while the succession of the race continues; just as the sun, wind, and rivers are ever shifting about, while the cycle in which they move is invariable; they return to the point whence they set out. Hence is man, as in these objects of nature which are his analogue, with all the seeming changes "there is no new thing" (Ec 1:9).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Maurer translates, "All words are wearied out," that is, are inadequate, as also, "man cannot express" all the things in the world which undergo this ceaseless, changeless cycle of vicissitudes: "The eye is not satisfied with seeing them," &c. But it is plainly a return to the idea (Ec 1:3) as to man's "labor," which is only wearisome and profitless; "no new" good can accrue from it (Ec 1:9); for as the sun, &c., so man's laborious works move in a changeless cycle. The eye and ear are two of the taskmasters for which man toils. But these are never "satisfied" (Ec 6:7; Pr 27:20). Nor can they be so hereafter, for there will be nothing "new." Not so the chief good, Jesus Christ (Joh 4:13, 14; Re 21:5).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Rather, "no new thing at all"; as in Nu 11:6. This is not meant in a general sense; but there is no new source of happiness (the subject in question) which can be devised; the same round of petty pleasures, cares, business, study, wars, &c., being repeated over and over again [Holden].

entries.title: Isaiah 13:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. old time-- Hebrew, "ages." which was-- The Hebrew plural cannot be joined to the verb singular. Therefore translate: "It hath been in the ages before; certainly it hath been before us" [Holden]. Or, as Maurer: "That which has been (done) before us (in our presence, 1Ch 16:33), has been (done) already in the old times."

entries.title: Isaiah 13:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. The reason why some things are thought "new," which are not really so, is the imperfect record that exists of preceding ages among their successors. those that ... come after-- that is, those that live still later than the "things, rather the persons or generations, Ec 1:4, with which this verse is connected, the six intermediate verses being merely illustrations of Ec 1:4 [Weiss], that are to come" (Ec 2:16; 9:5).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Resumption of Ec 1:1, the intermediate verses being the introductory statement of his thesis. Therefore, "the Preacher" (Koheleth) is repeated. was king-- instead of "am," because he is about to give the results of his past experience during his long reign. in Jerusalem-- specified, as opposed to David, who reigned both in Hebron and Jerusalem; whereas Solomon reigned only in Jerusalem. "King of Israel in Jerusalem," implies that he reigned over Israel and Judah combined; whereas David, at Hebron, reigned only over Judah, and not, until he was settled in Jerusalem, over both Israel and Judah.

entries.title: Isaiah 13:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. this sore travail-- namely, that of "searching out all things done under heaven." Not human wisdom in general, which comes afterwards (Ec 2:12, &c.), but laborious enquiries into, and speculations about, the works of men; for example, political science. As man is doomed to get his bread, so his knowledge, by the sweat of his brow (Ge 3:19) [Gill]. exercised-- that is, disciplined; literally, "that they may thereby chastise, or humble themselves."

entries.title: Isaiah 13:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. The reason is here given why investigation into man's "works" is only "sore travail" (Ec 1:13); namely, because all man's ways are vain (Ec 1:18) and cannot be mended (Ec 1:15). vexation of-- "a preying upon" the Spirit-- Maurer translates; "the pursuit of wind," as in Ec 5:16; Ho 12:1, "Ephraim feedeth on wind." But old versions support the English Version.

entries.title: Isaiah 13:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Investigation (Ec 1:13) into human ways is vain labor, for they are hopelessly "crooked" and "cannot be made straight" by it (Ec 7:13). God, the chief good, alone can do this (Is 40:4; 45:2). wanting-- (Da 5:27). numbered-- so as to make a complete number; so equivalent to "supplied" [Maurer]. Or, rather, man's state is utterly wanting; and that which is wholly defective cannot be numbered or calculated. The investigator thinks he can draw up, in accurate numbers, statistics of man's wants; but these, including the defects in the investigator's labor, are not partial, but total.

entries.title: Isaiah 13:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. communed with ... heart-- (Ge 24:45). come to great estate-- Rather, "I have magnified and gotten" (literally, "added," increased), &c. all ... before me in Jerusalem-- namely, the priests, judges, and two kings that preceded Solomon. His wisdom exceeded that of all before Jesus Christ, the antitypical Koheleth, or "Gatherer of men," (Lu 13:34), and "Wisdom" incarnate (Mt 11:19; 12:42). had ... experience-- literally, "had seen" (Jr 2:31). Contrast with this glorying in worldly wisdom (Jr 9:23, 24).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. wisdom ... madness-- that is, their effects, the works of human wisdom and folly respectively. "Madness," literally, "vaunting extravagance"; Ec 2:12; 7:25, &c., support English Version rather than Dathe, "splendid matters." "Folly" is read by English Version with some manuscripts, instead of the present Hebrew text, "prudence." If Hebrew be retained, understand "prudence," falsely so called (1Ti 6:20), "craft" (Da 8:25).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. wisdom ... knowledge-- not in general, for wisdom, &c., are most excellent in their place; but speculative knowledge of man's ways (Ec 1:13, 17), which, the farther it goes, gives one the more pain to find how "crooked" and "wanting" they are (Ec 1:15; 12:12).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 2 Ec 2:1-26. He next tries pleasure and luxury, retaining however, his worldly "wisdom" (Ec 3:9), but all proves "vanity" in respect to the chief good. 1. I said ... heart-- (Lu 12:19). thee-- my heart, I will test whether thou canst find that solid good in pleasure which was not in "worldly wisdom." But this also proves to be "vanity" (Is 50:11).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. laughter-- including prosperity, and joy in general (Job 8:21). mad-- that is, when made the chief good; it is harmless in its proper place. What doeth it?-- Of what avail is it in giving solid good? (Ec 7:6; Pr 14:13).

entries.title: Isaiah 13:21–14:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-11. Illustration more at large of Ec 2:1, 2. I sought-- I resolved, after search into many plans. give myself unto wine-- literally, "to draw my flesh," or "body to wine" (including all banquetings). Image from a captive drawn after a chariot in triumph (Ro 6:16, 19; 1Co 12:2); or, one "allured" (2Pe 2:18, 19). yet acquainting ... wisdom-- literally, "and my heart (still) was behaving, or guiding itself," with wisdom [Gesenius]. Maurer translates: "was weary of (worldly) wisdom." But the end of Ec 2:9 confirms English Version. folly-- namely, pleasures of the flesh, termed "mad," Ec 2:2. all the days, &c.-- (See Margin and Ec 6:12; Job 15:20).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. He had tried (worldly) wisdom (Ec 1:12-18) and folly (foolish pleasure) (Ec 2:1-11); he now compares them (Ec 2:12) and finds that while (worldly) wisdom excelleth folly (Ec 2:13, 14), yet the one event, death, befalls both (Ec 2:14-16), and that thus the wealth acquired by the wise man's "labor" may descend to a "fool" that hath not labored (Ec 2:18, 19, 21); therefore all his labor is vanity (Ec 2:22, 23). what can the man do ... already done-- (Ec 1:9). Parenthetical. A future investigator can strike nothing out "new," so as to draw a different conclusion from what I draw by comparing "wisdom and madness." Holden, with less ellipsis, translates, "What, O man, shall come after the king?" &c. Better, Grotius, "What man can come after (compete with) the king in the things which are done?" None ever can have the same means of testing what all earthly things can do towards satisfying the soul; namely, worldly wisdom, science, riches, power, longevity, all combined.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-14. (Pr 17:24). The worldly "wise" man has good sense in managing his affairs, skill and taste in building and planting, and keeps within safe and respectable bounds in pleasure, while the "fool" is wanting in these respects ("darkness," equivalent to fatal error, blind infatuation), yet one event, death, happens to both (Job 21:26).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. why was I-- so anxious to become, &c. (2Ch 1:10). Then-- Since such is the case. this-- namely, pursuit of (worldly) wisdom; it can never fill the place of the true wisdom (Job 28:28; Jr 8:9).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. remembrance-- a great aim of the worldly (Ge 11:4). The righteous alone attain it (Psa 112:6; Pr 10:7). for ever-- no perpetual memorial. that which now is-- Maurer, "In the days to come all things shall be now long ago forgotten."

entries.title: Isaiah 14:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. Disappointed in one experiment after another, he is weary of life. The backslider ought to have rather reasoned as the prodigal (Ho 2:6, 7; Lu 15:17, 18). grievous unto me-- (Job 10:1).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-19. One hope alone was left to the disappointed worldling, the perpetuation of his name and riches, laboriously gathered, through his successor. For selfishness is mostly at the root of worldly parents' alleged providence for their children. But now the remembrance of how he himself, the piously reared child of David, had disregarded his father's dying charge (1Ch 28:9), suggested the sad misgivings as to what Rehoboam, his son by an idolatrous Ammonitess, Naamah, should prove to be; a foreboding too fully realized (1Ki 12:1-18; 14:21-31).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. I gave up as desperate all hope of solid fruit from my labor.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. Suppose "there is a man," &c. equity-- rather "with success," as the Hebrew is rendered (Ec 11:6), "prosper," though Margin gives "right" [Holden and Maurer]. evil-- not in itself, for this is the ordinary course of things, but "evil," as regards the chief good, that one should have toiled so fruitlessly.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. Same sentiment as in Ec 2:21, interrogatively.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. The only fruit he has is, not only sorrows in his days, but all his days are sorrows, and his travail (not only has griefs connected with it, but is itself), grief.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. English Version gives a seemingly Epicurean sense, contrary to the general scope. The Hebrew, literally is, "It is not good for man that he should eat," &c., "and should make his soul see good" (or "show his soul, that is, himself, happy"), &c. [Weiss]. According to Holden and Weiss, Ec 3:12, 22 differ from this verse in the text and meaning; here he means, "It is not good that a man should feast himself, and falsely make as though his soul were happy"; he thus refers to a false pretending of happiness acquired by and for one's self; in Ec 3:12, 22; 5:18, 19, to real seeing, or finding pleasure when God gives it. There it is said to be good for a man to enjoy with satisfaction and thankfulness the blessings which God gives; here it is said not to be good to take an unreal pleasure to one's self by feasting, &c. This also I saw-- I perceived by experience that good (real pleasure) is not to be taken at will, but comes only from the hand of God [Weiss] (Psa 4:6; Is 57:19-21). Or as Holden, "It is the appointment from the hand of God, that the sensualist has no solid satisfaction" (good).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. hasten-- after indulgences (Pr 7:23; 19:2), eagerly pursue such enjoyments. None can compete with me in this. If I, then, with all my opportunities of enjoyment, failed utterly to obtain solid pleasure of my own making, apart from God, who else can? God mercifully spares His children the sad experiment which Solomon made, by denying them the goods which they often desire. He gives them the fruits of Solomon's experience, without their paying the dear price at which Solomon bought it.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. True, literally, in the Jewish theocracy; and in some measure in all ages (Job 27:16, 17; Pr 13:22; 28:8). Though the retribution be not so visible and immediate now as then, it is no less real. Happiness even here is more truly the portion of the godly (Psa 84:11; Mt 5:5; Mr 10:29, 30; Ro 8:28; 1Ti 4:8). that he-- the sinner may give-- that is, unconsciously and in spite of himself. The godly Solomon had satisfaction in his riches and wisdom, when God gave them (2Ch 1:11, 12). The backsliding Solomon had no happiness when he sought it in them apart from God; and the riches which he heaped up became the prey of Shishak (2Ch 12:9).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 3 Ec 3:1-22. Earthly pursuits are no doubt lawful in their proper time and order (Ec 3:1-8), but unprofitable when out of time and place; as for instance, when pursued as the solid and chief good (Ec 3:9, 10); whereas God makes everything beautiful in its season, which man obscurely comprehends (Ec 3:11). God allows man to enjoy moderately and virtuously His earthly gifts (Ec 3:12, 13). What consoles us amidst the instability of earthly blessings is, God's counsels are immutable (Ec 3:14). 1. Man has his appointed cycle of seasons and vicissitudes, as the sun, wind, and water (Ec 1:5-7). purpose-- as there is a fixed "season" in God's "purposes" (for example, He has fixed the "time" when man is "to be born," and "to die," Ec 3:2), so there is a lawful "time" for man to carry out his "purposes" and inclinations. God does not condemn, but approves of, the use of earthly blessings (Ec 3:12); it is the abuse that He condemns, the making them the chief end (1Co 7:31). The earth, without human desires, love, taste, joy, sorrow, would be a dreary waste, without water; but, on the other hand, the misplacing and excess of them, as of a flood, need control. Reason and revelation are given to control them.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. time to die-- (Psa 31:15; He 9:27). plant-- A man can no more reverse the times and order of "planting," and of "digging up," and transplanting, than he can alter the times fixed for his "birth" and "death." To try to "plant" out of season is vanity, however good in season; so to make earthly things the chief end is vanity, however good they be in order and season. Gill takes it, not so well, figuratively (Jr 18:7, 9; Am 9:15; Mt 15:13).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. time to kill-- namely, judicially, criminals; or, in wars of self-defense; not in malice. Out of this time and order, killing is murder. to heal-- God has His times for "healing" (literally, Is 38:5, 21; figuratively, De 32:39; Ho 6:1; spiritually, Psa 147:3; Is 57:19). To heal spiritually, before the sinner feels his wound, would be "out of time," and so injurious. time to break down-- cities, as Jerusalem, by Nebuchadnezzar. build up-- as Jerusalem, in the time of Zerubbabel; spiritually (Am 9:11), "the set time" (Psa 102:13-16).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. mourn-- namely, for the dead (Ge 23:2). dance-- as David before the ark (2Sa 6:12-14; Psa 30:11); spiritually (Mt 9:15; Lu 6:21; 15:25). The Pharisees, by requiring sadness out of time, erred seriously.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. cast away stones-- as out of a garden or vineyard (Is 5:2). gather-- for building; figuratively, the Gentiles, once castaway stones, were in due time made parts of the spiritual building (Ep 2:19, 20), and children of Abraham (Mt 3:9); so the restored Jews hereafter (Psa 102:13, 14; Zec 9:16). refrain ... embracing-- (Joe 2:16; 1Co 7:5, 6).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. time to get-- for example, to gain honestly a livelihood (Ep 4:23). lose-- When God wills losses to us, then is our time to be content. keep-- not to give to the idle beggar (2Th 3:10). cast away-- in charity (Pr 11:24); or to part with the dearest object, rather than the soul (Mr 9:43). To be careful is right in its place, but not when it comes between us and Jesus Christ (Lu 10:40-42).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. rend-- garments, in mourning (Joe 2:13); figuratively, nations, as Israel from Judah, already foretold, in Solomon's time (1Ki 11:30, 31), to be "sewed" together hereafter (Eze 37:15, 22). silence-- (Am 5:13), in a national calamity, or that of a friend (Job 2:13); also not to murmur under God's visitation (Le 10:3; Psa 39:1, 2, 9).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. hate-- for example, sin, lusts (Lu 14:26); that is, to love God so much more as to seem in comparison to hate "father or mother," when coming between us and God. a time of war ... peace-- (Lu 14:31).

entries.title: Isaiah 14:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. But these earthly pursuits, while lawful in their season, are "unprofitable" when made by man, what God never intended them to be, the chief good. Solomon had tried to create an artificial forced joy, at times when he ought rather to have been serious; the result, therefore, of his labor to be happy, out of God's order, was disappointment. "A time to plant" (Ec 3:2) refers to his planting (Ec 2:5); "laugh" (Ec 3:4), to Ec 2:1, 2; "his mirth," "laughter"; "build up," "gather stones" (Ec 3:3, 5), to his "building" (Ec 2:4); "embrace," "love," to his "princess" (see on Ec 2:8); "get" (perhaps also "gather," Ec 3:5, 6), to his "gathering" (Ec 2:8). All these were of "no profit," because not in God's time and order of bestowing happiness.

entries.title: Isaiah 14:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Isaiah 15 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. his time-- that is, in its proper season (Psa 1:3), opposed to worldlings putting earthly pursuits out of their proper time and place (see on Ec 3:9). set the world in their heart-- given them capacities to understand the world of nature as reflecting God's wisdom in its beautiful order and times (Ro 1:19, 20). "Everything" answers to "world," in the parallelism. so that-- that is, but in such a manner that man only sees a portion, not the whole "from beginning to end" (Ec 8:17; Job 26:14; Ro 11:33; Re 15:4). Parkhurst, for "world," translates: "Yet He hath put obscurity in the midst of them," literally, "a secret," so man's mental dimness of sight as to the full mystery of God's works. So Holden and Weiss. This incapacity for "finding out" (comprehending) God's work is chiefly the fruit of the fall. The worldling ever since, not knowing God's time and order, labors in vain, because out of time and place.

entries.title: Isaiah 15:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. in them-- in God's works (Ec 3:11), as far as relates to man's duty. Man cannot fully comprehend them, but he ought joyfully to receive ("rejoice in") God's gifts, and "do good" with them to himself and to others. This is never out of season (Ga 6:9, 10). Not sensual joy and self-indulgence (Php 4:4; Jas 4:16, 17).

entries.title: Isaiah 15:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Literally, "And also as to every man who eats ... this is the gift of God" (Ec 3:22; 5:18). When received as God's gifts, and to God's glory, the good things of life are enjoyed in their due time and order (Ac 2:46; 1Co 10:31; 1Ti 4:3, 4).

entries.title: Isaiah 15:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. (1Sa 3:12; 2Sa 23:5; Psa 89:34; Mt 24:35; Jas 1:17). for ever-- as opposed to man's perishing labors (Ec 2:15-18). any thing taken from it-- opposed to man's "crooked and wanting" works (Ec 1:15; 7:13). The event of man's labors depends wholly on God's immutable purpose. Man's part, therefore, is to do and enjoy every earthly thing in its proper season (Ec 3:12, 13), not setting aside God's order, but observing deep reverence towards God; for the mysteriousness and unchangeableness of God's purposes are designed to lead "man to fear before Him." Man knows not the event of each act: otherwise he would think himself independent of God.

entries.title: Isaiah 15:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Resumption of Ec 1:9. Whatever changes there be, the succession of events is ordered by God's "everlasting" laws (Ec 3:14), and returns in a fixed cycle. requireth that ... past-- After many changes, God's law requires the return of the same cycle of events, as in the past, literally, "that which is driven on." The Septuagint and Syriac translate: "God requireth (that is, avengeth) the persecuted man"; a transition to Ec 3:16, 17. The parallel clauses of the verse support English Version.

entries.title: Isaiah 15:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. Here a difficulty is suggested. If God "requires" events to move in their perpetual cycle, why are the wicked allowed to deal unrighteously in the place where injustice ought least of all to be; namely, "the place of judgment" (Jr 12:1)?

entries.title: Isaiah 15:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. Solution of it. There is a coming judgment in which God will vindicate His righteous ways. The sinner's "time" of his unrighteous "work" is short. God also has His "time" and "work" of judgment; and, meanwhile, is overruling, for good at last, what seems now dark. Man cannot now "find out" the plan of God's ways (Ec 3:11; Psa 97:2). If judgment instantly followed every sin, there would be no scope for free will, faith, and perseverance of saints in spite of difficulties. The previous darkness will make the light at last the more glorious. there-- (Job 3:17-19) in eternity, in the presence of the Divine Judge, opposed to the "there," in the human place of judgment (Ec 3:16): so "from thence" (Ge 49:24).

entries.title: Isaiah 15:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. estate-- The estate of fallen man is so ordered (these wrongs are permitted), that God might "manifest," that is, thereby prove them, and that they might themselves see their mortal frailty, like that of the beasts. sons of men-- rather, "sons of Adam," a phrase used for "fallen men." The toleration of injustice until the judgment is designed to "manifest" men's characters in their fallen state, to see whether the oppressed will bear themselves aright amidst their wrongs, knowing that the time is short, and there is a coming judgment. The oppressed share in death, but the comparison to "beasts" applies especially to the ungodly oppressors (Psa 49:12, 20). They too need to be "manifested" ("proved"), whether, considering that they must soon die as the "beasts," and fearing the judgment to come, they will repent (Da 4:27).

entries.title: Isaiah 15:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. Literally, "For the sons of men (Adam) are a mere chance, as also the beast is a mere chance." These words can only be the sentiments of the skeptical oppressors. God's delay in judgment gives scope for the "manifestation" of their infidelity (Ec 8:11; Psa 55:19; 2Pe 3:3,4). They are "brute beasts," morally (Ec 3:18; Jude 10); and they end by maintaining that man, physically, has no pre-eminence over the beast, both alike being "fortuities." Probably this was the language of Solomon himself in his apostasy. He answers it in Ec 3:21. If Ec 3:19, 20 be his words, they express only that as regards liability to death, excluding the future judgment, as the skeptic oppressors do, man is on a level with the beast. Life is "vanity," if regarded independently of religion. But Ec 3:21 points out the vast difference between them in respect to the future destiny; also (Ec 3:17) beasts have no "judgment" to come. breath-- vitality.

entries.title: Isaiah 16 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. Who knoweth-- Not doubt of the destination of man's spirit (Ec 12:7); but "how few, by reason of the outward mortality to which man is as liable as the beast and which is the ground of the skeptic's argument, comprehend the wide difference between man and the beast" (Is 53:1). The Hebrew expresses the difference strongly, "The spirit of man that ascends, it belongeth to on high; but the spirit of the beast that descends, it belongeth to below, even to the earth." Their destinations and proper element differ utterly [Weiss].

entries.title: Isaiah 16:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. (Compare Ec 3:12; 5:18). Inculcating a thankful enjoyment of God's gifts, and a cheerful discharge of man's duties, founded on fear of God; not as the sensualist (Ec 11:9); not as the anxious money-seeker (Ec 2:23; 5:10-17). his portion-- in the present life. If it were made his main portion, it would be "vanity" (Ec 2:1; Lu 16:25). for who, &c.-- Our ignorance as to the future, which is God's "time" (Ec 3:11), should lead us to use the present time in the best sense and leave the future to His infinite wisdom (Mt 6:20, 25, 31-34).

entries.title: Isaiah 16:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 4 Ec 4:1-16. 1. returned-- namely, to the thought set forth (Ec 3:16; Job 35:9). power-- Maurer, not so well, "violence." no comforter-- twice said to express continued suffering without any to give comfort (Is 53:7).

entries.title: Isaiah 16:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. A profane sentiment if severed from its connection; but just in its bearing on Solomon's scope. If religion were not taken into account (Ec 3:17, 19), to die as soon as possible would be desirable, so as not to suffer or witness "oppressions"; and still more so, not to be born at all (Ec 7:1). Job (Job 3:12; 21:7), David (Psa 73:3, &c.), Jeremiah (Jr 12:1), Habakkuk (Ha 1:13), all passed through the same perplexity, until they went into the sanctuary, and looked beyond the present to the "judgment" (Psa 73:17; Ha 2:20; 3:17, 18). Then they saw the need of delay, before completely punishing the wicked, to give space for repentance, or else for accumulation of wrath (Ro 2:15); and before completely rewarding the godly, to give room for faith and perseverance in tribulation (Psa 92:7-12).
